(i).label: Q2.1(i)
(i).synopsis: Risks covered, Benefits payable, Term differences, CI lump sum vs IP income, Repeated claims, Survival period
(i).question: Compare the risks covered and the benefits available under a stand-alone critical illness insurance and an income protection insurance policy purchased by an individual.

Risks — Income Protection (IP): IP insurance covers the risk of being unable to earn a living as a result of injury or sickness.
This risk is covered throughout the fixed term of the policy or until the policyholder's death if that occurs earlier. Repeated claims are possible.
The term of an IP insurance contract does not extend beyond the policyholder's normal retirement age. The term of a CI insurance policy could extend beyond normal retirement age.
The cover ends if the policyholder dies before an insured event occurs. In this respect CI and IP insurance policies are similar.
Risks — Critical Illness (CI): CI insurance covers the risk of being diagnosed with one of the conditions or undergoing one of the surgical procedures listed in the policy, during the term of the policy.
Usually only the first occurrence of one of these conditions is covered and cover ceases once a claim is paid.
However, under some CI insurance policies (e.g. tiered benefits or with buy-back options), cover may continue after claim.
Benefits — Income Protection (IP): IP insurance provides a regular income starting at the end of the deferred period specified in the policy and continuing until the policyholder is able to work again, dies or the term of the policy expires, whichever event occurs first.
The amount of the regular income is limited to the smaller of a proportion (specified in the policy) of the pre-incapacity income or the sum insured in the policy.
Benefits in payment may increase at a rate specified in the policy.
A reduced benefit may be paid if policyholders return to work on a part-time basis, or accepts employment at a lower salary than they were receiving before becoming incapacitated.

If the policy conditions prescribe it, the qualifying conditions for the payment of benefits may change after an initial benefit period (e.g. from an 'own occupation' definition of work to an 'any occupation to which the insured is suited by education and training' definition).
Benefits — Critical Illness (CI): CI insurance usually provides the payment of a single lump sum specified in the policy.
The benefit payment is typically the full sum insured, but under tiered benefit plans only a proportion of the sum insured may be payable on claim.
There is often a survival period specified in the policy, e.g. the policyholder must live for 28 days after diagnosis of the disease before the sum insured is paid.
For some policies the sum insured (and the premiums) may be linked to an inflation index.

In some circumstances it may be possible to commute regular IP payments for a lump sum (e.g. in the case of a terminal illness) or to take lump sum CI insurance policy benefits in the form of a regular income.

(ii)(a).label: Q2.1(ii)(a)
(ii)(a).synopsis: No dependants, greatest risk is inability to work, IP more appropriate, deferred period, two-thirds salary, inflation protection, waiver of premium, stress illnesses, redundancy not covered
(ii)(a).question: Describe, with reasons, which of these insurance policies might be suitable for a single life aged 25, with no children, who is a member of an employer-sponsored money purchase stakeholder pension scheme, in which the employer matches the employee's contribution.

Risk Profile: They are exposed to the risks of dying, being unable to work because of sickness or injury and being made redundant from their job.

They have no immediate dependants, and there will be no immediate losses if they die. Any loans can be repaid from their estate, which will receive the fund value of their money purchase pension and the proceeds from the sale of any assets.
The greatest financial risk they face is likely to be that of being unable to work, and so being unable to earn a living, as a result of sickness or injury.
Recommendation — IP Insurance: Their employer may provide statutory and discretionary sick pay for several months. They should therefore choose an IP policy with a deferred period equal to the length of time for which they expect to receive statutory and discretionary sick pay. The term of the policy should extend to their expected normal retirement age.
The IP policy should have a level monthly income benefit equal to about two-thirds of their salary.
They should take care that the level of their income benefit does not exceed the income replacement ratio used by the insurer to limit benefit payments.

To guard against the risk of failing health in the future, they could choose a policy with an option that allows increases to the benefit amount to match future increases in salary without providing additional evidence of health.
To guard against a long-term benefit whose value is eroded by price inflation, they should choose a policy for which the benefit amount increases in (and possibly also out of) payment.
A policy that waives premiums while benefits were being paid would be preferable to one with no premium waiver.
If we consider the types of condition this young person is expected to suffer compared to those of an older person, IP insurance may be considered to be more appropriate than CI insurance.
Conditions that occur frequently at younger ages, such as stress-related illnesses, might lead to IP payments but are unlikely to be serious enough to lead to CI payments.

The majority of serious illnesses that a young person would expect to suffer, leading to a CI payment, would also be expected to lead to payment of IP benefits, because the insured would be expected to be off work for a long time (e.g. for cancer treatment).
Possible Case for CI Insurance: However, the lump sum benefit from a CI policy offers more flexibility for the insured following a claim, compared with the regular income from an IP policy.
This flexibility may be more important to younger policyholders, and so a CI policy may be preferred.
If the insured changes occupation or place of residence, the insurer might revise the level of cover and/or premiums. This is more likely to affect IP policies than CI policies.
This might be particularly important if the insured ceases to be gainfully employed (e.g. takes a career break).
Limitations of Both Policies: Neither IP nor CI insurance will cover the risk of redundancy.
A version of IP insurance called accident, sickness and unemployment (ASU) insurance would do this. But unlike IP insurance it would only pay benefits for a limited period.

(ii)(b).label: Q2.1(ii)(b)
(ii)(b).synopsis: DB scheme covers death and ill-health, IP duplicates DB scheme, CI fills gaps, CI more flexible, employer sick pay reduces need for IP, redundancy not covered
(ii)(b).question: Describe, with reasons, which of these insurance policies might be suitable for a married life aged 35 with a dependent spouse and two children aged 7 and 5, who is a member of a non-contributory defined benefit occupational pension scheme with generous benefits.

Risk Profile: They are exposed to the risks of dying, being unable to work because of sickness or injury and being made redundant from their job.
The defined benefit pension scheme is likely to cover the risk of dying by providing a lump sum on death (e.g. four times salary at death) ...
... and a dependants' pension for a spouse and children (e.g. up to two thirds of salary at the date of death).
The defined benefit pension scheme is likely to cover the risk that they are permanently unable to continue working by providing an ill-health retirement pension for the rest of their life.
Case for IP Insurance (Partial): However, the risk of temporary periods of being unable to work is not covered by the defined benefit pension scheme. This could be covered by an IP insurance policy.
Benefits from an IP policy would provide income during periods of temporary absence from work as a result of sickness or injury.
However, IP insurance would duplicate some of the ill-health retirement benefits from the pension scheme.
Recommendation — CI Insurance: The disruption to family life caused by a serious illness might in part be addressed by CI insurance. This would not duplicate the benefits provided by the pension scheme.

The CI insurance provides a very flexible benefit — for example, the sum insured could pay for medical treatment, convalescence, one-off family expenses, pay off personal loans or even provide a holiday.
On balance CI insurance should take precedence over IP insurance, which would cover fewer additional risks not already covered by membership of the pension scheme.
This is particularly true if the employer provides short-term sickness benefits related to salary.
As was the case for the life aged 25, neither insurance will cover the risk of redundancy.
